title: Cat Retained Baby Teeth (Double Fangs): A Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Wondering why your cat has double fangs? Learn about retained baby teeth, when to see a vet, and how photos help with dental triage.
content_html:
What are Retained Baby Teeth?

Retained deciduous teeth, commonly known as "double fangs," occur when a kitten's primary teeth fail to fall out as the permanent adult teeth emerge. This is most frequently seen with the upper canine teeth. Normally, the root of the baby tooth should be absorbed by the body, allowing it to fall out easily; however, when this process fails, both teeth occupy the same socket or sit side-by-side.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for retained baby teeth is Low. This is not an immediate medical emergency that requires an ER visit. However, it does require a scheduled appointment with your primary veterinarian. If these teeth are not removed, they can cause overcrowding, trap bacteria and food, lead to premature periodontal disease, or force the adult teeth into an abnormal position that could cause pain or injury to the roof of the mouth.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a clear photo of your cat's mouth allows a veterinary professional to see the alignment of the teeth and the health of the surrounding gums. A photo helps them determine if there is significant inflammation or if the adult tooth is being displaced. This information helps the vet decide if the extraction should be performed immediately or if it can wait until your cat's scheduled spay or neuter procedure.

faqs.question: Will the baby tooth eventually fall out on its own?
faqs.answer: While some baby teeth may fall out late, if the adult tooth is already fully visible and the baby tooth is not loose, it is unlikely to fall out without veterinary assistance.

faqs.question: At what age should all baby teeth be gone?
faqs.answer: Most kittens should have all their permanent adult teeth by 6 to 7 months of age.

faqs.question: How are retained teeth usually treated?
faqs.answer: The standard treatment is surgical extraction under general anesthesia, often performed at the same time as a spay or neuter to avoid multiple sessions of anesthesia.
